title: Elattostachys xylocarpa
intro: Elattostachys xylocarpa, commonly known as the Australian cherry, is a species of flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, which may help to alleviate symptoms of fever, cough, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Elattostachys xylocarpa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared into infusions, decoctions, or extracts, and is commonly used to treat respiratory and gastrointestinal disorders, as well as to support immune function.
taxonomy: Elattostachys xylocarpa is classified as a species within the family Sapindaceae, commonly known as the soapberry or lychee family. This family consists of approximately 140 genera and 1,500 species, primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions of the world. Elattostachys xylocarpa is a genus of trees in the Sapindaceae family. The Sapindaceae family is part of the larger order Sapindales, which also includes other families such as the Anacardiaceae and the Meliaceae. The classification of Elattostachys xylocarpa reflects its botanical relationships and evolutionary history within the Sapindaceae family.
uses: Elattostachys xylocarpa is reportedly used in traditional medicine, particularly in its leaves and bark. It is believed to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its medicinal uses.
